here
on May 9th. So, let's unpack each of the
six key macro cycles in order.
We'll start with the growth cycle. Macro
weather model is currently generating a
neutral market risk assessment for the
growth cycle. We got some negative data
particularly from the side of non-farm
productivity growth this week. We think
it's a transitory slowdown, but if we're
wrong and if it's not a transitory
slowdown, then I think it just, you
know, creates a lot more, you know, left
tail. It removes some of the right tail
risk that we've been highlighting in our
research really since going back to last
April and you know, after we
correctly forecasted the crash of Q1 of
last year and then we very quickly
pivoted to being bullish
about as quick as anybody out there back
in April of last year. So, we've been
forecasting a tremendous amount of right
tail risk in the economy primarily
through the lens of our productivity
paradigm C theme, but then we layered on
our productivity boom theme, our job
recovery theme, our cooling housing
labor theme, and all those themes in our
opinion are are what are the primary
reason why you know, the markets are
where they are from a levels perspective
and performance perspective. And so, you
know, I think we've been right about a
lot of stuff in I've been right in
years. We've been right about a lot of
stuff in the last five plus years since
we started this company. So,

inflation cycle. Macro world model is

headwind uh in the inflation cycle.

impulse of 3.5%. Core PCE price deflator
has got a strong positive impulse of
4.4%. The CRB index has got a strong
positive impulse of 15%. Five-year

strong negative impulse of 2.4%. The
consensus short-run potential headline
CPI estimate has got a strong positive
impulse at 2.8%. Uh monetary policy,
macro world model is currently signaling
a modest tailwind for the monetary
policy cycle. We have a weak easing
impulse in the Fed funds rate. We have a
strong tightening impulse in the
two-year nominal Treasury yield Fed
funds rate spread. Uh we got a uh weak
easing impulse uh in the Fed Treasury
holdings to marketable Treasury debt
ratio. We have a strong tightening
impulse in the bank reserves to
commercial bank assets ratio. And then
we got a weak easing impulse in the SOFR
IRB spread. Fiscal policy, we got a
meaningful tailwind in fiscal policy.
If you look at the uh we got a strong
tightening impulse in the trailing
12-month fiscal sovereign fiscal balance
to GDP ratio at uh minus 5.2%. We have a
uh strong easing impulse in the trailing
12-month federal revenue at 6%. Uh we
got a trailing uh 12 uh strong easing
impulse in trailing 12-month federal
expenditures at 3%. We got a Treasury
general account balance to bank reserves
ratio. Uh we got a weak easing impulse
uh in that statistic at 30%. We have a
strong easing impulse in the bills to
marketable Treasury debt ratio uh at
22%. Transitioning to the liquidity
cycle, we got a modest tailwind in the
liquidity cycle, strong positive impulse
in global liquidity.
We got a weak positive impulse in US
liquidity. We got a weak tightening
impulse in the move index, weak easing
impulse on the term and 10-year Treasury
term premium. And then we got a weak
tightening impulse in the US dollar
index. And then finally positioning
cycle, we got a meaningful headwind in
the positioning cycle. If you look at
the top five positioning model
indicators ranked by the degree of
dispersion that they did demonstrate in
major bull market peaks and bear market
troughs. You know, four of those five
indicators are currently breaching their
bubble peak means when you look at the
everything bubble peak from Jan 2022,
the housing bubble peak from October of
'07 and then the dot-com bubble peak
from March of 2000. So, you know, when
you amalgamate the the the influence of
each of these six key macro cycles which
you know

signals of each asset class on an
independent basis,
you know, we're still in a scenario
where the Bayesian prior, the stuff that
matters to investors, the people who
move the most money around in the world,
folks managing trillions of dollars of
capital, they care about this.

And so the stuff that they care about
are generally on on net moving in a in a

If the that'll obviously cause the
meaningful headwind in the inflation
cycle to stay to remain a meaningful
headwind. We don't expect it to remain a
meaningful headwind over the medium
term, but it'll stay a meaningful
headwind if we're wrong on this rate of
our moves dynamic. And then ultimately,
if you have the liquidity cycle
deflecting to a headwind, inflation
cycle staying a meaningful headwind,
then you're going to lose the tailwind
in the monetary policy cycle. And if you
lose the tailwind in the monetary policy
cycle and the liquidity cycle and the
inflation cycle stays in a meaningful
headwind, then you're going to go from
neutral in the growth cycle, which is
currently we expect it to go back to a
tailwind, you're actually going to go in
the wrong direction uh to a headwind.
And so, that's how you could start, you
know, causing real negative outcomes in
financial markets in the context of this
crowded bullish positioning as indicated
by the meaningful headwind in the
positioning cycle. So, that is the risk.
That is the risk, but I I you know,
tongue in cheek, I was joking on on on

from the perspective of the 60 macro
cycles and some of the things that are
currently headwinds, particularly the

think they're doable as we'll talk about
uh later in this presentation. Uh we use
our grid model to assess the probability
of regime change in asset markets over a
medium to long-term time horizon. So,
we're still above consensus on growth,
we're still below consensus on core PCE
inflation. Those are two very positive
dynamics if we're right on that if the
model is right on that. And by the way,
the model knows nothing about our
generally positive uh views on the
growth cycle from a thematic standpoint.
And the model knows very uh knows very
little about our generally positive
views on the inflation cycle from a
thematic standpoint. And the model is
essentially saying, just based on the
auto regressive properties of these time
series, the probability that that growth
surprises to the upside and inflation
surprises to the downside is is is high.

in the context of those themes. Uh that
